# Transcrição: 06-Course Q&As

- **Fonte:** 06-Course Q&As
- **Duração:** 05:16
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Your course questions answered. In this lesson we're going to cover some of the most common questions about course creation and marketing. So let's hit it. First of all, question number one, how long should my sessions be? Research shows that the sweet spot is about 12 to 20 minutes. If they are much shorter than that, it can be disjointed. Imagine having 23 minute lessons. That can be really confusing and hard for your student to move through. If you have a lot of shorter lessons, think about grouping several topics together. And if your lessons are coming in significantly longer, think about breaking them up. Throughout Mastermind Business System, you'll see that some lessons might be slightly longer or shorter and that's okay. There really are no hard and fast rules, but experience shows that about 12 to 20 minutes is a sweet spot for adult learners online. Question number two, how long should my whole course be? There are no hard and fast rules for this one either. The transformation is more important than the length. More when it comes to courses is not always better. People will pay for speed and the shorter your course is, the more likely people will go through all the material. At the same time, if you have one single 12 minute lesson and you're asking $5,000 for it, there might be a disconnect between the perceived value and the length of your course. People might not trust that you can deliver what you say you can in just one 12 minute session. There is a balance there and you may need to test it out. Question number three, how much should I charge for my course? You took an initial pass at pricing in the Irresistible Product Framework section, but let's dive back into that now. First of all, ask how valuable is the transformation you are providing? If you have a course to teach people to save $500 per month on their family's grocery bill, it's easy to justify asking for $200 for the course. For our Wardrobe Consultant Joy, she might calculate that her students will save $1,000 a year on clothing they buy and don't wear, so she might think $100 for her course is a great price point. For some of us, the dollar calculation isn't as clear cut. Like if you are teaching self-empowerment or how to golf better, it can be hard to quantify the value in terms of dollars. In these cases, you can do some market research, which is covered in a later lesson, in a later section. You can ask questions like, are there other courses like yours? How much do they charge for a similar outcome? How is your course more or less complete? You can also ask, is your topic something that people can learn on their own? If so, how hard would it be for them to piece together their own curriculum? And how many hours will your course save them? For instance, people can learn to code through free resources online, but let's say your course will teach them

## [03:00 - 05:16]

the basics needed to land their first coding job in just 10 hours. That's a valuable outcome. Also, do you have testimonials or success stories from students? If you have a lot of success stories, you can justify your price more easily because you've been proven to get the outcome you're offering. Another question is, do you provide additional support, like a community or live question and answer sessions? Remember though, your course is worth what you're confident asking. There are courses on everything under the sun and some are free and some are top dollar. So start with an amount you're comfortable with or maybe just ask a little bit more than you feel comfortable with and you can adjust it from there. The only wrong decision is to not launch at all. Question number four, what about recording? I do not want to go live on camera. We've got a great lesson on how to easily record your course in a number of different ways, but even after watching that, if you still don't want to be on camera, do a voiceover like I'm doing for this lesson. I specifically did this so you could see how you can create a lesson without ever being on camera. You can also hire someone else to present your course for you. You can go to sites like Upwork.com to find spokespeople or you can even hire an AI-generated spokesperson. But you know what? I really urge you to present your own content. No one knows it and loves it like you do. Question number five, I love Dean's story teach tool model. How does that fit into the process you taught us? Great question. Story teach tool is so versatile and it fits perfectly into this structure. Story can be the example. Then you teach and tell what the story means. Then the tool can be the tactics or blueprint you provide. Remember Joy in her wardrobe course? She could tell a story, which is the example of one of her clients. Then she can teach about why what the client did worked so well. And then as a tool, she can give a checklist. Voila. Thanks for tuning in. I have loved spending this time with you and in the next lesson, you're going to learn how easy it is to record and edit your video. And then you'll also have some step-by-step tutorials on uploading your course into the Mastermind Business Hub and creating a sales page so you can get your amazing course out into the world. You rock.
